impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury's finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel-vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust damages award, rejecting Ingevity's defense that its honeycombs were nonstaple goods protected from antitrust liability under patent law. The district court had found whether the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question, and the jury found against Ingevity.
impacts.positiveImplications: Reinforces the principle that antitrust counterclaims can serve as a powerful defense for competitors facing patent-leveraged exclusionary conduct, providing a pathway for businesses harmed by tying arrangements to recover damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition licenses on exclusive purchasing of unpatented products face significant antitrust liability risk, even when the tied products are closely related to the patented technology. Companies must carefully structure patent licensing to avoid tying claims.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact preclude summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent procurement and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on fraud before the PTO.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims in a dispute over coiled tubing patents. The court found genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether Tenaris committed inequitable conduct by failing to disclose prior CYMAX technology documents to the PTO during patent prosecution, and whether that conduct rose to the level of Walker Process fraud supporting an attempted monopolization claim. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of inequitable conduct during patent prosecution have the opportunity for full factual proceedings rather than summary resolution, and the high bar for Walker Process fraud remains intact.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies acquiring prior art assets and subsequently obtaining related patents face increased litigation risk if they fail to disclose prior art to the PTO. The survival of both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims to trial creates significant exposure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statutory framework governing trade investigations. The court also held that Section 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to records of judicial proceedings. The decision affirms the Court of International Trade's order directing the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements rather than applying automatic designations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Increases transparency in trade remedy proceedings, potentially benefiting importers and other interested parties who can access information previously shielded by automatic confidentiality designations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit detailed business information in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of public disclosure. Domestic producers and respondents in trade investigations may need to be more proactive in specifically designating and justifying confidential treatment for sensitive business data.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content delivery specifications to the screen size and capabilities of a requesting wireless device are eligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming systems for tailoring streaming media content specifications to wireless device capabilities are ineligible for patenting under § 101. The court reversed the district court's judgment of infringement of the '715 patent and its $2.5 million damages award to GoTV, entering judgment for Netflix. The court also reversed the indefiniteness holding on the '865 patent but found all asserted claims patent-ineligible.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides a significant defense for streaming and content delivery companies against patent assertions directed to content adaptation for different devices, reinforcing § 101 as a barrier to abstract software patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to adaptive content delivery and device-tailored streaming face heightened invalidity risk under § 101, potentially reducing the value of patent portfolios in the streaming technology space.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing IPR non-institution discretion are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court reasoned that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions merely guide the Board as the Director's delegatee, and the Director retains authority to make the final non-institution decision. This preserves the PTO's ability to issue and modify IPR institution guidance without formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO Director's retained flexibility to set IPR institution policy, as discretionary denials can shield issued patents from IPR challenges, particularly in cases involving parallel district court litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and IPR petitioners lose their challenge to require formal rulemaking for IPR institution guidelines, meaning the PTO can continue to restrict access to IPR proceedings through informal policy changes without public input.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development s.r.o. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly granted judgment as a matter of law overturning a jury verdict of trade secret misappropriation and $64 million in damages related to self-inflating tire technology, and whether correction of patent inventorship was properly denied.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of JMOL overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory, $61.2M punitive) for trade secret misappropriation related to self-inflating tire technology. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship on Goodyear's patent. The decision involved detailed analysis of five alleged trade secrets and found insufficient evidence to sustain the jury's findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides reassurance to companies that collaborate with outside parties on technology development that JMOL remains an effective tool to overturn unsupported trade secret verdicts, even large ones with significant punitive damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret plaintiffs face a high evidentiary bar to sustain misappropriation verdicts, particularly when the alleged trade secrets are defined broadly and the evidence of actual misuse is contested.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are eligible subject matter under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether such claims can be dismissed at the pleading stage under Rule 12(b)(6).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the dismissal of a patent infringement complaint against Google at the pleading stage, finding that claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities were patent-ineligible under § 101. The ruling confirms that § 101 challenges can be resolved on a motion to dismiss when the patent claims are directed to abstract ideas without an inventive concept.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from early-stage dismissal of infringement suits based on § 101 ineligibility, reducing the cost of defending against weak patent assertions, particularly those involving abstract software concepts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting software-related patents face increasing risk of pleading-stage dismissal under § 101, further constraining the enforceability of patents directed to web-based collaboration and streaming technologies.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claim terms in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's obviousness analysis was properly supported under the correct claim construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR proceeding involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision resolved a disputed claim construction that the Board majority had adopted over a dissent, siding with Netflix's interpretation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming service providers and content delivery platforms benefit from the court's willingness to correct PTAB claim constructions that could otherwise sustain patents covering fundamental streaming and encryption technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content and DRM space face risk that favorable PTAB decisions may be reversed on appeal when claim construction is contested, creating uncertainty in IPR outcomes.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulation requiring written authorization for overtime pay (5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c)) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a), which requires the government to pay for overtime 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: In an en banc decision, the Federal Circuit upheld the OPM regulation requiring written authorization for federal employee overtime pay, finding the writing requirement is a valid exercise of OPM's rulemaking authority. The court affirmed dismissal of the overtime claim (Count II) of IHS nurses who worked extensive overtime during COVID-19 without written authorization, but referred remaining claims regarding nighttime, Sunday, and holiday pay back to the panel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from confirmation that the longstanding written overtime authorization requirement remains valid, providing a clear administrative mechanism to control overtime costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees who work overtime without formal written authorization may be unable to recover overtime pay even when supervisors were aware of the work, creating particular hardship for healthcare workers and others in high-demand emergency situations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS registered nurses hired under Title 5 but paid under Title 38 base pay provisions are entitled to enhanced compensation for nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 or Title 5 regulations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit panel reversed dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for nighttime, Sunday, holiday, and miscellaneous overtime work during the COVID-19 pandemic. The court held that nurses paid under Title 38 special salary provisions may state viable claims for additional compensation under both Title 38 § 7453 and Title 5 pay regulations, remanding for further proceedings on these claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential backpay liability for failing to apply Title 38 pay enhancements to Title 5 nurses receiving Title 38 base pay, with potential class-wide implications for IHS and similar agencies employing hybrid Title 5/Title 38 healthcare workers.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement of spinal surgery device patents and in granting JMOL to the defendant.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and the grant of JMOL in favor of DePuy in a case involving induced infringement of spinal derotation surgery patents. The court found the district court abused its discretion in excluding Dr. Yassir's and Dr. Neal's testimony and remanded for a new trial where both experts may testify. The case involves significant medical device patents covering en bloc derotation techniques.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting medical device patents benefit from the court's enforcement of proper Daubert standards, ensuring that relevant expert testimony on infringement is not improperly excluded.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed induced infringement exposure when expert testimony exclusion is reversed, particularly in cases involving surgical techniques where the line between induced and direct infringement depends heavily on expert opinions about surgical practice.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB properly cancelled a trademark registration and dismissed an opposition proceeding based on common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement executed after the opposition was filed.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' trademark registration and dismissed Game Plan's opposition to Uninterrupted IP's marks. UNIP successfully established prior common law rights through a 2019 asset purchase agreement that transferred both the 'MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' mark and associated goodwill from a party that had used the mark since 2012. The decision reinforces that acquired common law rights, including goodwill, can be used to establish priority in TTAB proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies can strengthen trademark positions by acquiring common law rights and goodwill through asset purchases, even after opposition proceedings have commenced, providing strategic flexibility in brand disputes.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants may find their registrations vulnerable to cancellation based on common law rights that a competitor acquires after litigation begins, requiring careful monitoring of potential competing prior uses.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over a district court order imposing an $8 million bond under the Idaho Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act on patent assertion entities.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed for lack of jurisdiction an appeal from an $8 million bond imposed on patent assertion entities under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act. The court found that the bond order was not a final decision amenable to appeal. The underlying case involves Idaho's state law targeting abusive patent assertion practices, which allows targets of bad faith patent infringement claims to seek bonds, damages, and equitable relief.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies and other targets of patent assertion entities benefit from state-level anti-troll legislation remaining in force, as the jurisdictional dismissal leaves the bond requirement intact pending further proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face continued exposure to state anti-troll statutes that can impose significant financial bonds before litigation proceeds, though the lack of immediate appellate review creates procedural uncertainty.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an agreement between a government contractor and the government regarding the inclusion of 'Drag' costs in overhead pools for cost-plus contracts is valid, and whether the Board's decision on overhead allocation base constitutes a final appealable decision.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held it lacked jurisdiction over the Board's non-final decision regarding the overhead cost allocation base in Pratt & Whitney's government contracts, but found jurisdiction over the 'Drag' cost issue under the collateral order doctrine. The court reversed the Board's finding that an enforceable agreement existed regarding inclusion of Drag costs in overhead pools, holding the agreement invalid. The case involves complex cost accounting disputes in defense contracting.
impacts.positiveImplications: Government agencies benefit from invalidation of agreements that could inflate overhead cost pools in cost-plus contracts, potentially saving significant defense procurement costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors face increased scrutiny of collaboration agreements with suppliers and the resulting cost allocations to government contracts. The invalidation of cost-sharing agreements may require restructuring of supplier relationships and overhead accounting practices.

impacts.caseName: Adnexus Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent infringement complaint, supported by appended claim charts and preliminary infringement contentions, stated a plausible claim for relief sufficient to survive a motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the dismissal of Adnexus's patent infringement complaint against Meta's Lead Ads product and remanded for further proceedings. The court found that Adnexus's amended complaint, which included claim charts and preliminary infringement contentions mapping patent limitations onto Meta's product, was sufficient to state a plausible claim for relief. This decision clarifies the pleading standard for patent infringement cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a clearer standard that detailed claim charts and infringement contentions appended to complaints can satisfy pleading requirements, making it harder for defendants to obtain early dismissal of infringement suits.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies face reduced ability to obtain early dismissal of patent infringement complaints when plaintiffs provide detailed claim charts, even if the underlying infringement theories may be disputed.
